items.q: What is the behaviourist view on the nature-nurture debate?
items.correct: Strongly nurture - we are shaped by environmental conditioning.
items.why: Behaviourists reject innate ideas (tabula rasa - blank slate). Students soften this to 'a mix'. Watson and Skinner explicitly attributed nearly all behaviour to environment.

items.q: What does a correlation of -0.8 indicate?
items.misconception: A weak relationship (because it is negative).
items.why: Students treat the negative sign as 'weak'. The sign shows DIRECTION (as one variable goes up, the other goes down); the absolute value shows STRENGTH. -0.8 is strong.

items.q: Asch's line study primarily demonstrates which type of conformity?
items.correct: Normative social influence (compliance to fit in).
items.misconception: Informational social influence (believing others know better).
items.why: Students assume participants genuinely believed the wrong line was correct. Asch's debriefs showed they knew it was wrong but conformed publicly to avoid rejection - this is normative, not informational.
Correct answer: Worry and being on edge. “Joy” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The answer examiners want is: Noticing and using feelings. “Maths” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: If you can name 30 feelings rather than 5, how many extra?
items.why: Exact answer: 25. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
